chunks.text: After starting or up-titrating an ACE inhibitor (lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril) or angiotensin receptor blocker (losartan, valsartan, candesartan), recheck serum potassium and creatinine within 1 to 2 weeks. Reasonable laboratory tolerances are a serum creatinine rise of up to about 30 percent above baseline and a serum potassium up to 5.5 mEq/L; values within these bounds typically reflect expected hemodynamic effects on the renal efferent arteriole rather than intrinsic injury. A creatinine increase greater than 30 percent above baseline, or a potassium of 5.5 mEq/L or higher, warrants holding the agent and investigating volume status, concurrent NSAID or potassium-sparing diuretic use, dietary potassium load, and the possibility of bilateral renal artery stenosis.
chunks.title: ACE inhibitor and ARB monitoring — potassium, creatinine, and adverse effects (AHA)

After resolution, restart at a lower dose with closer monitoring; do not co-prescribe an ACE inhibitor with an ARB, and avoid combining either with direct renin inhibitors.

chunks.text: Angioedema is a rare but life-threatening adverse effect. It can occur months or years after therapy initiation and is more common in Black patients. Suspect angioedema with any acute facial, lip, tongue, or glottic swelling: discontinue the agent permanently, avoid all angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, and use ARBs only with caution given a small cross-reactivity rate.

chunks.text: Atrial fibrillation is the most common sustained arrhythmia in adults and roughly quintuples stroke risk when untreated. The 2023 ACC/AHA/ ACCP/HRS atrial-fibrillation guideline recommends estimating stroke risk with the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and offering oral anticoagulation for any score of 2 or higher in men and 3 or higher in women. A score of 1 in men and 2 in women is a shared-decision indication; patient-level bleeding risk and preferences determine whether anticoagulation is started.
chunks.title: Atrial fibrillation — anticoagulation, stroke risk, and bleeding (AHA)

chunks.text: The 2023 ACC/AHA/ ACCP/HRS atrial-fibrillation guideline recommends estimating stroke risk with the CHA₂DS₂-VASc score and offering oral anticoagulation for any score of 2 or higher in men and 3 or higher in women. A score of 1 in men and 2 in women is a shared-decision indication; patient-level bleeding risk and preferences determine whether anticoagulation is started. CHA₂DS₂-VASc components are congestive heart failure, hypertension, age 75 or older (2 points), diabetes, stroke or TIA (2 points), vascular disease, age 65 to 74, and female sex.
chunks.title: Atrial fibrillation — anticoagulation, stroke risk, and bleeding (AHA)

chunks.text: A score of 1 in men and 2 in women is a shared-decision indication; patient-level bleeding risk and preferences determine whether anticoagulation is started. CHA₂DS₂-VASc components are congestive heart failure, hypertension, age 75 or older (2 points), diabetes, stroke or TIA (2 points), vascular disease, age 65 to 74, and female sex. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) — apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, and dabigatran — are first-line for non-valvular atrial fibrillation in most patients.

chunks.text: CHA₂DS₂-VASc components are congestive heart failure, hypertension, age 75 or older (2 points), diabetes, stroke or TIA (2 points), vascular disease, age 65 to 74, and female sex. Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) — apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, and dabigatran — are first-line for non-valvular atrial fibrillation in most patients. Apixaban 5 mg twice daily is the typical dose, with dose reduction to 2.5 mg twice daily when two of three criteria are met: age 80 or older, weight 60 kg or less, or serum creatinine 1.5 mg/dL or higher.

chunks.text: Direct oral anticoagulants (DOACs) — apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, and dabigatran — are first-line for non-valvular atrial fibrillation in most patients. Apixaban 5 mg twice daily is the typical dose, with dose reduction to 2.5 mg twice daily when two of three criteria are met: age 80 or older, weight 60 kg or less, or serum creatinine 1.5 mg/dL or higher. Warfarin is preferred for moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valves; INR target is 2 to 3 for most indications.

Warfarin is preferred for moderate-to-severe mitral stenosis or mechanical heart valves; INR target is 2 to 3 for most indications. Bleeding-risk stratification with HAS-BLED supports clinician judgment but does not by itself contraindicate anticoagulation: hypertension, abnormal renal or hepatic function, prior stroke, prior bleeding, labile INR, age above 65, antiplatelet/NSAID use, and alcohol excess.

chunks.text: After acute coronary syndrome, the foundation of secondary prevention is dual antiplatelet therapy, a beta blocker, a high-intensity statin, and — when indicated — an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker. Dual antiplatelet therapy combines aspirin 81 mg daily with a P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel, ticagrelor, or prasugrel). The standard duration after acute coronary syndrome or coronary stenting is 12 months, with shorter regimens (1 to 6 months) considered in patients at high bleeding risk and longer courses considered in patients at high ischemic risk and acceptable bleeding risk.
chunks.title: Coronary artery disease — secondary prevention pharmacotherapy after MI (AHA)

chunks.text: Dual antiplatelet therapy combines aspirin 81 mg daily with a P2Y12 inhibitor (clopidogrel, ticagrelor, or prasugrel). The standard duration after acute coronary syndrome or coronary stenting is 12 months, with shorter regimens (1 to 6 months) considered in patients at high bleeding risk and longer courses considered in patients at high ischemic risk and acceptable bleeding risk. After the dual antiplatelet course completes, aspirin 81 mg daily continues indefinitely.

chunks.text: The standard duration after acute coronary syndrome or coronary stenting is 12 months, with shorter regimens (1 to 6 months) considered in patients at high bleeding risk and longer courses considered in patients at high ischemic risk and acceptable bleeding risk. After the dual antiplatelet course completes, aspirin 81 mg daily continues indefinitely. Beta blockade with a cardioselective agent (metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol, carvedilol) is initiated early after myocardial infarction and continued for at least 12 months.

Beta blockade with a cardioselective agent (metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol, carvedilol) is initiated early after myocardial infarction and continued for at least 12 months. In patients with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, beta blockade continues indefinitely.

chunks.text: Beta blockade with a cardioselective agent (metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol, carvedilol) is initiated early after myocardial infarction and continued for at least 12 months. In patients with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, beta blockade continues indefinitely. Choose metoprolol succinate (extended-release) over metoprolol tartrate when long-term once-daily dosing and ejection-fraction protection are the goals.

chunks.text: In patients with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction, beta blockade continues indefinitely. Choose metoprolol succinate (extended-release) over metoprolol tartrate when long-term once-daily dosing and ejection-fraction protection are the goals. High-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 40 to 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20 to 40 mg) is started in-hospital or at discharge and continued indefinitely, targeting an LDL reduction of 50 percent or greater and an LDL below 70 mg/dL.

chunks.text: The 2018 ACC/AHA cholesterol guideline groups patients into four statin-benefit categories. Patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) — prior myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke, transient ischemic attack of atherosclerotic origin, peripheral arterial disease, or coronary or other arterial revascularization — are candidates for high-intensity statin therapy regardless of baseline LDL cholesterol. High-intensity statins lower LDL by approximately 50 percent or more and include atorvastatin 40 or 80 mg daily and rosuvastatin 20 or 40 mg daily.
chunks.title: Cholesterol management — statin intensity by ASCVD risk (AHA)

chunks.text: Patients with clinical atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease (ASCVD) — prior myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke, transient ischemic attack of atherosclerotic origin, peripheral arterial disease, or coronary or other arterial revascularization — are candidates for high-intensity statin therapy regardless of baseline LDL cholesterol. High-intensity statins lower LDL by approximately 50 percent or more and include atorvastatin 40 or 80 mg daily and rosuvastatin 20 or 40 mg daily. For primary prevention, an LDL cholesterol of 190 mg/dL or higher is itself an indication for high-intensity statin therapy without formal risk calculation.
chunks.title: Cholesterol management — statin intensity by ASCVD risk (AHA)

chunks.text: High-intensity statins lower LDL by approximately 50 percent or more and include atorvastatin 40 or 80 mg daily and rosuvastatin 20 or 40 mg daily. For primary prevention, an LDL cholesterol of 190 mg/dL or higher is itself an indication for high-intensity statin therapy without formal risk calculation. Adults aged 40 to 75 with diabetes are candidates for at least moderate-intensity statin therapy regardless of estimated risk; high-intensity statin is reasonable when ASCVD risk is elevated or the patient has multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
chunks.title: Cholesterol management — statin intensity by ASCVD risk (AHA)

chunks.text: For primary prevention, an LDL cholesterol of 190 mg/dL or higher is itself an indication for high-intensity statin therapy without formal risk calculation. Adults aged 40 to 75 with diabetes are candidates for at least moderate-intensity statin therapy regardless of estimated risk; high-intensity statin is reasonable when ASCVD risk is elevated or the patient has multiple cardiovascular risk factors. For other adults aged 40 to 75 without diabetes and with LDL between 70 and 189 mg/dL, the 10-year ASCVD risk score (Pooled Cohort Equations) drives the decision: borderline risk (5 to 7.5 percent) favors lifestyle plus consideration of moderate-intensity statin if risk-enhancing factors are present (family history of premature ASCVD, chronic kidney disease, metabolic syndrome, persistent inflammatory disease, or non-traditional lipid markers); intermediate risk (7.5 to 20 percent) favors moderate-intensity statin therapy; high risk (20 percent or higher) favors high-intensity statin.

chunks.text: Adults aged 40 to 75 with diabetes are candidates for at least moderate-intensity statin therapy regardless of estimated risk; high-intensity statin is reasonable when ASCVD risk is elevated or the patient has multiple cardiovascular risk factors. For other adults aged 40 to 75 without diabetes and with LDL between 70 and 189 mg/dL, the 10-year ASCVD risk score (Pooled Cohort Equations) drives the decision: borderline risk (5 to 7.5 percent) favors lifestyle plus consideration of moderate-intensity statin if risk-enhancing factors are present (family history of premature ASCVD, chronic kidney disease, metabolic syndrome, persistent inflammatory disease, or non-traditional lipid markers); intermediate risk (7.5 to 20 percent) favors moderate-intensity statin therapy; high risk (20 percent or higher) favors high-intensity statin. LDL response is reassessed 4 to 12 weeks after initiation and after dose changes, then every 3 to 12 months at steady state.

chunks.text: For other adults aged 40 to 75 without diabetes and with LDL between 70 and 189 mg/dL, the 10-year ASCVD risk score (Pooled Cohort Equations) drives the decision: borderline risk (5 to 7.5 percent) favors lifestyle plus consideration of moderate-intensity statin if risk-enhancing factors are present (family history of premature ASCVD, chronic kidney disease, metabolic syndrome, persistent inflammatory disease, or non-traditional lipid markers); intermediate risk (7.5 to 20 percent) favors moderate-intensity statin therapy; high risk (20 percent or higher) favors high-intensity statin. LDL response is reassessed 4 to 12 weeks after initiation and after dose changes, then every 3 to 12 months at steady state. In patients on high-intensity statin therapy who do not achieve at least a 50 percent LDL reduction or whose LDL remains above 70 mg/dL with very high ASCVD risk, ezetimibe is added; PCSK9 inhibitors may be considered when LDL remains above goal on maximally-tolerated statin plus ezetimibe.

chunks.text: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction is defined by a left ventricular ejection fraction of 40 percent or below with symptoms or signs of heart failure. The 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA heart-failure guideline identifies four foundational drug classes — collectively guideline- directed medical therapy, or GDMT — that are initiated and up-titrated to target dose in every HFrEF patient who tolerates them. The four classes are renin-angiotensin system inhibition (preferably an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor such as sacubitril-valsartan, otherwise an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker), an evidence-based beta blocker, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and an SGLT2 inhibitor.
chunks.title: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction — guideline-directed medical therapy (AHA)

chunks.text: The 2022 ACC/AHA/HFSA heart-failure guideline identifies four foundational drug classes — collectively guideline- directed medical therapy, or GDMT — that are initiated and up-titrated to target dose in every HFrEF patient who tolerates them. The four classes are renin-angiotensin system inhibition (preferably an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor such as sacubitril-valsartan, otherwise an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker), an evidence-based beta blocker, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Renin-angiotensin therapy: an ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan) is preferred over an ACE inhibitor or ARB when feasible and tolerable; switch from ACEi to ARNI requires a 36-hour washout to avoid angioedema.
chunks.title: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction — guideline-directed medical therapy (AHA)

chunks.text: The four classes are renin-angiotensin system inhibition (preferably an angiotensin receptor-neprilysin inhibitor such as sacubitril-valsartan, otherwise an ACE inhibitor or angiotensin receptor blocker), an evidence-based beta blocker, a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist, and an SGLT2 inhibitor. Renin-angiotensin therapy: an ARNI (sacubitril-valsartan) is preferred over an ACE inhibitor or ARB when feasible and tolerable; switch from ACEi to ARNI requires a 36-hour washout to avoid angioedema. In patients with documented ACE-inhibitor angioedema, do not re-challenge with another ACEi; an ARB (losartan, valsartan) can be substituted with caution because cross-reactivity is small but non-zero.

Beta blockade in HFrEF is restricted to one of the three agents proven to lower mortality: carvedilol, metoprolol succinate (extended release), or bisoprolol.

chunks.text: Mineralocorticoid receptor antagonists (spironolactone, eplerenone) are added in patients with NYHA class II to IV symptoms, ejection fraction at or below 35 percent, and adequate renal function and potassium. Initiation requires an eGFR above 30 mL/min/1.73 m² and a serum potassium below 5.0 mEq/L. Recheck potassium and creatinine at 2 to 4 weeks after start or dose change, then every 3 to 6 months.

chunks.text: The 2017 American College of Cardiology / American Heart Association hypertension guideline defines stage 1 hypertension as a systolic blood pressure of 130 to 139 mm Hg or a diastolic of 80 to 89 mm Hg, and stage 2 as 140/90 or higher. Diagnosis is established with at least two elevated office readings on separate occasions, ideally confirmed with home or ambulatory blood pressure monitoring. For most adults with hypertension, the recommended treatment target is a blood pressure below 130/80 mm Hg.
chunks.title: Hypertension management — blood pressure targets and first-line therapy (AHA)

chunks.text: Diagnosis is established with at least two elevated office readings on separate occasions, ideally confirmed with home or ambulatory blood pressure monitoring. For most adults with hypertension, the recommended treatment target is a blood pressure below 130/80 mm Hg. This target applies to patients with established cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and a 10-year atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk of 10 percent or higher.
chunks.title: Hypertension management — blood pressure targets and first-line therapy (AHA)

chunks.text: For most adults with hypertension, the recommended treatment target is a blood pressure below 130/80 mm Hg. This target applies to patients with established cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and a 10-year atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk of 10 percent or higher. Stage 1 hypertension in lower-risk adults is initially managed with lifestyle therapy (DASH-style diet, sodium reduction, weight loss, alcohol limitation, aerobic activity); stage 2 or high-risk stage 1 starts pharmacotherapy concurrently with lifestyle.

chunks.text: This target applies to patients with established cardiovascular disease, type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease, and a 10-year atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk of 10 percent or higher. Stage 1 hypertension in lower-risk adults is initially managed with lifestyle therapy (DASH-style diet, sodium reduction, weight loss, alcohol limitation, aerobic activity); stage 2 or high-risk stage 1 starts pharmacotherapy concurrently with lifestyle. First-line antihypertensive classes are thiazide-type diuretics (chlorthalidone, hydrochlorothiazide), calcium channel blockers (amlodipine), ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril), and angiotensin II receptor blockers (losartan, valsartan).

chunks.text: Acute otitis media is diagnosed by acute onset of symptoms, presence of middle-ear effusion, and signs of middle-ear inflammation (moderate-to-severe bulging of the tympanic membrane or otorrhea not caused by otitis externa). Mild bulging plus recent ear pain, or intense erythema of the tympanic membrane, also satisfy the inflammation criterion when otalgia is present. Initial antibiotic therapy is recommended for children aged 6 months or older with severe symptoms (moderate-to-severe otalgia, otalgia for at least 48 hours, or temperature 39°C or higher) and for children younger than 24 months with bilateral disease.
chunks.title: Acute otitis media — antibiotic selection and observation (CDC)

chunks.text: Mild bulging plus recent ear pain, or intense erythema of the tympanic membrane, also satisfy the inflammation criterion when otalgia is present. Initial antibiotic therapy is recommended for children aged 6 months or older with severe symptoms (moderate-to-severe otalgia, otalgia for at least 48 hours, or temperature 39°C or higher) and for children younger than 24 months with bilateral disease. For children aged 6 months to 23 months with non-severe unilateral disease, and children 24 months or older with non-severe disease in either ear, a 48 to 72 hour observation period with shared decision making and a safety-net prescription is acceptable.
chunks.title: Acute otitis media — antibiotic selection and observation (CDC)

chunks.text: Initial antibiotic therapy is recommended for children aged 6 months or older with severe symptoms (moderate-to-severe otalgia, otalgia for at least 48 hours, or temperature 39°C or higher) and for children younger than 24 months with bilateral disease. For children aged 6 months to 23 months with non-severe unilateral disease, and children 24 months or older with non-severe disease in either ear, a 48 to 72 hour observation period with shared decision making and a safety-net prescription is acceptable. First-line antibiotic is amoxicillin 80 to 90 mg per kg per day, divided into two doses, when the patient has not received amoxicillin in the prior 30 days, has no concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, and has no history of recurrent otitis media unresponsive to amoxicillin.

First-line antibiotic is amoxicillin 80 to 90 mg per kg per day, divided into two doses, when the patient has not received amoxicillin in the prior 30 days, has no concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, and has no history of recurrent otitis media unresponsive to amoxicillin. Amoxicillin-clavulanate at the same amoxicillin component dose is preferred when any of those conditions apply.

chunks.text: First-line antibiotic is amoxicillin 80 to 90 mg per kg per day, divided into two doses, when the patient has not received amoxicillin in the prior 30 days, has no concurrent purulent conjunctivitis, and has no history of recurrent otitis media unresponsive to amoxicillin. Amoxicillin-clavulanate at the same amoxicillin component dose is preferred when any of those conditions apply. Treatment duration is 10 days for children younger than 2 years and those with severe symptoms, 7 days for children aged 2 to 5 years with non-severe disease, and 5 to 7 days for children 6 years or older with non-severe disease.
chunks.title: Acute otitis media — antibiotic selection and observation (CDC)

chunks.text: Amoxicillin-clavulanate at the same amoxicillin component dose is preferred when any of those conditions apply. Treatment duration is 10 days for children younger than 2 years and those with severe symptoms, 7 days for children aged 2 to 5 years with non-severe disease, and 5 to 7 days for children 6 years or older with non-severe disease. Adults with acute otitis media are treated with amoxicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate at standard adult dosing for 5 to 7 days; penicillin allergy alternatives include cefdinir, cefuroxime, or — in true non-anaphylactic reactions — azithromycin, acknowledging higher pneumococcal resistance to macrolides.

Adults with acute otitis media are treated with amoxicillin or amoxicillin-clavulanate at standard adult dosing for 5 to 7 days; penicillin allergy alternatives include cefdinir, cefuroxime, or — in true non-anaphylactic reactions — azithromycin, acknowledging higher pneumococcal resistance to macrolides. Follow-up is guided by symptom course rather than fixed visit schedules; reassess at 48 to 72 hours when initial observation was elected, when symptoms persist or worsen, or when the diagnosis is in doubt.

chunks.text: CDC recommends that all adults receive an annual influenza vaccine, ideally in September or October. All licensed influenza vaccines appropriate for the patient's age and health status are acceptable. Pneumococcal vaccination is recommended for adults 65 years or older and for adults 19 to 64 years with certain medical conditions (chronic heart, lung, or liver disease; diabetes; immunocompromise; cigarette smoking).
chunks.title: Adult immunization schedule — routine vaccines (CDC)

chunks.text: All licensed influenza vaccines appropriate for the patient's age and health status are acceptable. Pneumococcal vaccination is recommended for adults 65 years or older and for adults 19 to 64 years with certain medical conditions (chronic heart, lung, or liver disease; diabetes; immunocompromise; cigarette smoking). Either PCV20 alone or PCV15 followed by PPSV23 is acceptable; current preference favors PCV20 for simplicity.

chunks.text: Iron-deficiency anemia in adults is most commonly screened with a complete blood count and ferritin. Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the single most useful test for diagnosing iron deficiency in the absence of inflammation. In adults without active inflammatory illness, a serum ferritin below approximately 30 ng/mL is consistent with iron deficiency; ferritin in the 30 to 100 ng/mL range may still indicate deficiency in patients with chronic disease, chronic kidney disease, or active inflammation, since ferritin is an acute-phase reactant.
chunks.title: Iron-deficiency anemia in adults — laboratory diagnosis and oral iron therapy (CDC)

chunks.text: Serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores and is the single most useful test for diagnosing iron deficiency in the absence of inflammation. In adults without active inflammatory illness, a serum ferritin below approximately 30 ng/mL is consistent with iron deficiency; ferritin in the 30 to 100 ng/mL range may still indicate deficiency in patients with chronic disease, chronic kidney disease, or active inflammation, since ferritin is an acute-phase reactant. A low ferritin with a low or low-normal transferrin saturation supports the diagnosis.
chunks.title: Iron-deficiency anemia in adults — laboratory diagnosis and oral iron therapy (CDC)

chunks.text: In adults without active inflammatory illness, a serum ferritin below approximately 30 ng/mL is consistent with iron deficiency; ferritin in the 30 to 100 ng/mL range may still indicate deficiency in patients with chronic disease, chronic kidney disease, or active inflammation, since ferritin is an acute-phase reactant. A low ferritin with a low or low-normal transferrin saturation supports the diagnosis. Initial work-up of newly identified iron deficiency in adults should include evaluation for menstrual blood loss in premenopausal women, gastrointestinal blood loss in adults at risk, dietary intake, and malabsorption (including celiac disease and prior gastric surgery).

Concurrent autoimmune thyroid disease can co-occur with iron deficiency and is worth noting on the differential. Oral iron is first-line for uncomplicated iron-deficiency anemia.

chunks.text: Concurrent autoimmune thyroid disease can co-occur with iron deficiency and is worth noting on the differential. Oral iron is first-line for uncomplicated iron-deficiency anemia. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) once daily, or every other day, is the typical adult regimen; alternate-day dosing improves fractional absorption and is generally better tolerated.

chunks.text: Oral iron is first-line for uncomplicated iron-deficiency anemia. Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) once daily, or every other day, is the typical adult regimen; alternate-day dosing improves fractional absorption and is generally better tolerated. Vitamin C (or an acidic juice) co-administered improves absorption.

chunks.text: Ferrous sulfate 325 mg (65 mg elemental iron) once daily, or every other day, is the typical adult regimen; alternate-day dosing improves fractional absorption and is generally better tolerated. Vitamin C (or an acidic juice) co-administered improves absorption. Calcium-containing supplements, antacids, proton pump inhibitors, levothyroxine, and tetracyclines should be separated from oral iron by at least 2 hours.

chunks.text: Vitamin C (or an acidic juice) co-administered improves absorption. Calcium-containing supplements, antacids, proton pump inhibitors, levothyroxine, and tetracyclines should be separated from oral iron by at least 2 hours. Reassess hemoglobin in 4 to 8 weeks; ferritin and transferrin saturation typically lag and are rechecked at 8 to 12 weeks.

Reassess hemoglobin in 4 to 8 weeks; ferritin and transferrin saturation typically lag and are rechecked at 8 to 12 weeks. Failure to respond to a trial of oral iron warrants evaluation for ongoing blood loss, malabsorption, or an alternative cause of anemia.

chunks.text: All adults should be asked about tobacco use, and adults who use tobacco should be advised to stop and offered both behavioral counseling and pharmacotherapy. The combined approach approximately doubles the likelihood of quit success compared to brief advice alone. First-line pharmacotherapy options include nicotine replacement therapy (patch, gum, lozenge, inhaler, or nasal spray), varenicline, and bupropion SR.
chunks.title: Tobacco use cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy (CDC / USPSTF)

chunks.text: The combined approach approximately doubles the likelihood of quit success compared to brief advice alone. First-line pharmacotherapy options include nicotine replacement therapy (patch, gum, lozenge, inhaler, or nasal spray), varenicline, and bupropion SR. Combination NRT (a long-acting patch with a short-acting form for breakthrough cravings) is more effective than single-form NRT.
chunks.title: Tobacco use cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy (CDC / USPSTF)

chunks.text: First-line pharmacotherapy options include nicotine replacement therapy (patch, gum, lozenge, inhaler, or nasal spray), varenicline, and bupropion SR. Combination NRT (a long-acting patch with a short-acting form for breakthrough cravings) is more effective than single-form NRT. Pregnant patients should be offered behavioral counseling; routine pharmacotherapy use during pregnancy is not recommended owing to limited safety data, though clinicians may consider NRT case by case when behavioral support alone is insufficient.
chunks.title: Tobacco use cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy (CDC / USPSTF)

chunks.text: Combination NRT (a long-acting patch with a short-acting form for breakthrough cravings) is more effective than single-form NRT. Pregnant patients should be offered behavioral counseling; routine pharmacotherapy use during pregnancy is not recommended owing to limited safety data, though clinicians may consider NRT case by case when behavioral support alone is insufficient. For adults with cardiovascular disease, tobacco cessation reduces the risk of recurrent cardiovascular events more than any single medication.
chunks.title: Tobacco use cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy (CDC / USPSTF)

chunks.text: Pregnant patients should be offered behavioral counseling; routine pharmacotherapy use during pregnancy is not recommended owing to limited safety data, though clinicians may consider NRT case by case when behavioral support alone is insufficient. For adults with cardiovascular disease, tobacco cessation reduces the risk of recurrent cardiovascular events more than any single medication. Cessation counseling should be offered at every clinical encounter.

chunks.text: Adults seen in primary care should be screened for unhealthy alcohol use at least annually. AUDIT-C is the recommended brief screen: three items totaling 0 to 12 points, with a positive screen at 4 or higher in men and 3 or higher in women. A positive AUDIT-C is followed by the full 10-item AUDIT or by direct clinical interview to characterize severity.
chunks.title: Unhealthy alcohol use and alcohol use disorder — screening, counseling, and pharmacotherapy (NIH/NIAAA)

chunks.text: AUDIT-C is the recommended brief screen: three items totaling 0 to 12 points, with a positive screen at 4 or higher in men and 3 or higher in women. A positive AUDIT-C is followed by the full 10-item AUDIT or by direct clinical interview to characterize severity. Standard-drink counts (one standard drink equals 14 g of ethanol — roughly 12 oz of regular beer, 5 oz of wine, or 1.5 oz of distilled spirits) anchor the conversation and the diagnostic thresholds: low-risk drinking is up to 14 drinks per week and 4 per day for men, and up to 7 per week and 3 per day for women; heavy-drinking days exceed those daily limits.
chunks.title: Unhealthy alcohol use and alcohol use disorder — screening, counseling, and pharmacotherapy (NIH/NIAAA)

Brief behavioral counseling for unhealthy alcohol use in primary care reduces drinking and is a USPSTF Grade B recommendation for adults.

Components include feedback on the patient's pattern, comparison to recommended limits, motivational interviewing, goal setting, and arranged follow-up.

Alcohol use disorder is diagnosed by DSM-5 criteria; the count of met criteria sets severity (mild 2 to 3, moderate 4 to 5, severe 6 or more). FDA-approved medications for alcohol use disorder are naltrexone (oral 50 mg daily or extended-release injection 380 mg monthly), acamprosate (666 mg three times daily), and disulfiram (250 to 500 mg daily).

chunks.text: Alcohol use disorder is diagnosed by DSM-5 criteria; the count of met criteria sets severity (mild 2 to 3, moderate 4 to 5, severe 6 or more). FDA-approved medications for alcohol use disorder are naltrexone (oral 50 mg daily or extended-release injection 380 mg monthly), acamprosate (666 mg three times daily), and disulfiram (250 to 500 mg daily). Naltrexone is first-line for most adults including those who continue drinking; it is contraindicated with current opioid use or acute hepatitis.

chunks.text: FDA-approved medications for alcohol use disorder are naltrexone (oral 50 mg daily or extended-release injection 380 mg monthly), acamprosate (666 mg three times daily), and disulfiram (250 to 500 mg daily). Naltrexone is first-line for most adults including those who continue drinking; it is contraindicated with current opioid use or acute hepatitis. Acamprosate is preferred when liver disease precludes naltrexone or when abstinence is the explicit goal.

chunks.text: Naltrexone is first-line for most adults including those who continue drinking; it is contraindicated with current opioid use or acute hepatitis. Acamprosate is preferred when liver disease precludes naltrexone or when abstinence is the explicit goal. Disulfiram requires committed abstinence and a supervised administration arrangement.
chunks.title: Unhealthy alcohol use and alcohol use disorder — screening, counseling, and pharmacotherapy (NIH/NIAAA)

chunks.text: Acamprosate is preferred when liver disease precludes naltrexone or when abstinence is the explicit goal. Disulfiram requires committed abstinence and a supervised administration arrangement. Alcohol-related liver disease ranges from steatosis to hepatitis to cirrhosis.
chunks.title: Unhealthy alcohol use and alcohol use disorder — screening, counseling, and pharmacotherapy (NIH/NIAAA)

chunks.text: Disulfiram requires committed abstinence and a supervised administration arrangement. Alcohol-related liver disease ranges from steatosis to hepatitis to cirrhosis. Laboratory pattern in alcohol-related hepatitis classically shows AST elevated more than ALT in a ratio of about 2 to 1, with both typically below 300 to 500 U/L.
chunks.title: Unhealthy alcohol use and alcohol use disorder — screening, counseling, and pharmacotherapy (NIH/NIAAA)

chunks.text: Alcohol-related liver disease ranges from steatosis to hepatitis to cirrhosis. Laboratory pattern in alcohol-related hepatitis classically shows AST elevated more than ALT in a ratio of about 2 to 1, with both typically below 300 to 500 U/L. Total bilirubin elevation, INR prolongation, and a Maddrey discriminant function above 32 identify patients with severe alcoholic hepatitis who may benefit from specialty evaluation.

chunks.text: Asthma management is staged by severity and control. The 2020 NAEPP focused update from NHLBI organizes therapy into six steps; the goals of treatment are to maintain normal activity, minimize symptoms, preserve lung function, and avoid exacerbations and side effects. Persistent moderate asthma is treated with a daily inhaled corticosteroid in combination with a long-acting beta agonist (ICS-LABA), commonly fluticasone-furoate / vilanterol or fluticasone-propionate / salmeterol, with as-needed short-acting bronchodilator (albuterol) for rescue.
chunks.title: Asthma — stepwise management and care during pregnancy (NIH/NHLBI)

chunks.text: The complete blood count reports concentrations of hemoglobin and hematocrit, total and differential leukocyte counts, platelet count, red cell indices including mean corpuscular volume (MCV), and red cell distribution width. Interpretation depends on the patient's baseline, clinical context, and the magnitude of any deviation, but laboratory-typical adult reference and action thresholds are useful anchors. Anemia is defined in adults as a hemoglobin below approximately 13 g/dL in men and below approximately 12 g/dL in non-pregnant women.
chunks.title: Complete blood count interpretation — common adult thresholds (NIH/NHLBI)

chunks.text: Interpretation depends on the patient's baseline, clinical context, and the magnitude of any deviation, but laboratory-typical adult reference and action thresholds are useful anchors. Anemia is defined in adults as a hemoglobin below approximately 13 g/dL in men and below approximately 12 g/dL in non-pregnant women. Hemoglobin below 10 g/dL is moderate anemia; below 8 g/dL is severe.
chunks.title: Complete blood count interpretation — common adult thresholds (NIH/NHLBI)

chunks.text: Anemia is defined in adults as a hemoglobin below approximately 13 g/dL in men and below approximately 12 g/dL in non-pregnant women. Hemoglobin below 10 g/dL is moderate anemia; below 8 g/dL is severe. The MCV partitions anemia into microcytic (MCV below 80 fL — iron deficiency, thalassemia trait, anemia of chronic disease in some cases), normocytic (MCV 80 to 100 fL — acute blood loss, anemia of chronic disease, early iron deficiency, hemolysis, marrow infiltration), and macrocytic (MCV above 100 fL — vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, hypothyroidism, alcohol-related, drug-induced including methotrexate or zidovudine).
chunks.title: Complete blood count interpretation — common adult thresholds (NIH/NHLBI)

chunks.text: Hemoglobin below 10 g/dL is moderate anemia; below 8 g/dL is severe. The MCV partitions anemia into microcytic (MCV below 80 fL — iron deficiency, thalassemia trait, anemia of chronic disease in some cases), normocytic (MCV 80 to 100 fL — acute blood loss, anemia of chronic disease, early iron deficiency, hemolysis, marrow infiltration), and macrocytic (MCV above 100 fL — vitamin B12 or folate deficiency, hypothyroidism, alcohol-related, drug-induced including methotrexate or zidovudine). White blood cell count above approximately 11,000 per microliter (11.0 × 10⁹/L) is leukocytosis; with a left shift toward neutrophils, this most commonly reflects acute infection, stress response, or corticosteroid effect.

White blood cell count above approximately 11,000 per microliter (11.0 × 10⁹/L) is leukocytosis; with a left shift toward neutrophils, this most commonly reflects acute infection, stress response, or corticosteroid effect. WBC above 30,000 per microliter, persistent neutrophilia without an obvious driver, or basophilia, eosinophilia, or circulating blast forms warrant hematology evaluation.

WBC above 30,000 per microliter, persistent neutrophilia without an obvious driver, or basophilia, eosinophilia, or circulating blast forms warrant hematology evaluation. Neutrophil percentage above 70 to 75 percent of total WBC supports a bacterial or acute inflammatory process; lymphocyte predominance is more typical of viral illness.

Neutrophil percentage above 70 to 75 percent of total WBC supports a bacterial or acute inflammatory process; lymphocyte predominance is more typical of viral illness. Platelet count below approximately 150,000 per microliter is thrombocytopenia.

chunks.text: Neutrophil percentage above 70 to 75 percent of total WBC supports a bacterial or acute inflammatory process; lymphocyte predominance is more typical of viral illness. Platelet count below approximately 150,000 per microliter is thrombocytopenia. Counts above 50,000 are typically asymptomatic; spontaneous bleeding risk rises below 20,000, and platelets below 10,000 are an indication for inpatient evaluation and possible transfusion.
chunks.title: Complete blood count interpretation — common adult thresholds (NIH/NHLBI)

chunks.text: Platelet count below approximately 150,000 per microliter is thrombocytopenia. Counts above 50,000 are typically asymptomatic; spontaneous bleeding risk rises below 20,000, and platelets below 10,000 are an indication for inpatient evaluation and possible transfusion. Sudden drops to under half of baseline or to under 100,000 within days warrant immediate work-up for drug-induced thrombocytopenia (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is a notable emergency), immune thrombocytopenia, sepsis, or microangiopathic hemolytic anemia.

chunks.text: Counts above 50,000 are typically asymptomatic; spontaneous bleeding risk rises below 20,000, and platelets below 10,000 are an indication for inpatient evaluation and possible transfusion. Sudden drops to under half of baseline or to under 100,000 within days warrant immediate work-up for drug-induced thrombocytopenia (heparin-induced thrombocytopenia is a notable emergency), immune thrombocytopenia, sepsis, or microangiopathic hemolytic anemia. Pancytopenia (low hemoglobin, low WBC, and low platelets simultaneously) always warrants prompt evaluation for marrow failure, infiltration, or acute systemic illness.

chunks.text: A standard fasting lipid panel reports total cholesterol, LDL cholesterol, HDL cholesterol, and triglycerides. Reference ranges commonly printed on U.S. lab reports include: * Total cholesterol: less than 200 mg/dL (desirable). * LDL cholesterol: less than 100 mg/dL (optimal); 130 mg/dL or higher is borderline-to-high and is one criterion for the dyslipidemia risk factor used in cardiovascular risk calculators. * HDL cholesterol: greater than 40 mg/dL in men and greater than 50 mg/dL in women is desirable; values below 40 mg/dL count as a cardiovascular risk factor. * Triglycerides: less than 150 mg/dL (normal). A non-HDL cholesterol value (total minus HDL) of 130 mg/dL or higher roughly tracks an LDL of 100 mg/dL or higher and is increasingly used as a treatment threshold in patients with elevated triglycerides where calculated LDL is unreliable.
chunks.title: Cholesterol thresholds and management overview (NHLBI)

chunks.text: Reference ranges commonly printed on U.S. lab reports include: * Total cholesterol: less than 200 mg/dL (desirable). * LDL cholesterol: less than 100 mg/dL (optimal); 130 mg/dL or higher is borderline-to-high and is one criterion for the dyslipidemia risk factor used in cardiovascular risk calculators. * HDL cholesterol: greater than 40 mg/dL in men and greater than 50 mg/dL in women is desirable; values below 40 mg/dL count as a cardiovascular risk factor. * Triglycerides: less than 150 mg/dL (normal). A non-HDL cholesterol value (total minus HDL) of 130 mg/dL or higher roughly tracks an LDL of 100 mg/dL or higher and is increasingly used as a treatment threshold in patients with elevated triglycerides where calculated LDL is unreliable. Initial management for borderline values is typically lifestyle modification (diet, physical activity, weight management); statin therapy is layered in based on overall cardiovascular risk per the USPSTF / AHA-ACC primary-prevention guidance.
chunks.title: Cholesterol thresholds and management overview (NHLBI)

chunks.text: Chronic kidney disease is staged by estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR), expressed in mL/min/1.73 m². Stage G1 corresponds to an eGFR of 90 or higher with evidence of kidney damage; G2 is 60 to 89 with evidence of damage; G3a is 45 to 59; G3b is 30 to 44; G4 is 15 to 29; and G5 is below 15 (kidney failure). Albuminuria adds a parallel dimension: A1 is below 30 mg/g, A2 is 30 to 300, and A3 is above 300 mg of albumin per gram of creatinine on a spot urine albumin-to- creatinine ratio.
chunks.title: Chronic kidney disease — staging by eGFR and management implications (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: Stage G1 corresponds to an eGFR of 90 or higher with evidence of kidney damage; G2 is 60 to 89 with evidence of damage; G3a is 45 to 59; G3b is 30 to 44; G4 is 15 to 29; and G5 is below 15 (kidney failure). Albuminuria adds a parallel dimension: A1 is below 30 mg/g, A2 is 30 to 300, and A3 is above 300 mg of albumin per gram of creatinine on a spot urine albumin-to- creatinine ratio. A confirmed eGFR of 60 or below for at least three months establishes chronic kidney disease independent of cause.
chunks.title: Chronic kidney disease — staging by eGFR and management implications (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: Albuminuria adds a parallel dimension: A1 is below 30 mg/g, A2 is 30 to 300, and A3 is above 300 mg of albumin per gram of creatinine on a spot urine albumin-to- creatinine ratio. A confirmed eGFR of 60 or below for at least three months establishes chronic kidney disease independent of cause. Repeat the measurement to confirm — a single low eGFR can reflect acute illness, dehydration, recent contrast exposure, or laboratory variability.

Repeat the measurement to confirm — a single low eGFR can reflect acute illness, dehydration, recent contrast exposure, or laboratory variability. BUN-to-creatinine ratio interpretation is supplementary; isolated BUN elevation often reflects volume depletion, gastrointestinal bleeding, or high-protein intake rather than intrinsic renal disease.

These agents reduce intraglomerular pressure and slow CKD progression independent of their blood-pressure effect. SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) further reduce CKD progression in patients with eGFR above 20 and albuminuria, and are now recommended in CKD with or without diabetes.

chunks.text: The comprehensive metabolic panel reports sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, blood urea nitrogen (BUN), creatinine, glucose, calcium, total protein, albumin, total bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, AST, and ALT. The panel anchors evaluation of acid-base status, renal function, hepatic function, and metabolic state. Serum sodium below 135 mEq/L is hyponatremia.
chunks.title: Comprehensive metabolic panel interpretation — electrolytes, kidney, and liver (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: The panel anchors evaluation of acid-base status, renal function, hepatic function, and metabolic state. Serum sodium below 135 mEq/L is hyponatremia. Mild hyponatremia (130 to 134) is often dilutional in the outpatient setting, related to thiazides, SSRIs, or excess water intake; sodium below 125 with neurologic symptoms is a medical emergency requiring careful correction at no more than 8 to 10 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination.
chunks.title: Comprehensive metabolic panel interpretation — electrolytes, kidney, and liver (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: Serum sodium below 135 mEq/L is hyponatremia. Mild hyponatremia (130 to 134) is often dilutional in the outpatient setting, related to thiazides, SSRIs, or excess water intake; sodium below 125 with neurologic symptoms is a medical emergency requiring careful correction at no more than 8 to 10 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination. Hypernatremia (sodium above 145) most commonly reflects free-water deficit and is corrected with hypotonic fluid at a similarly cautious rate.
chunks.title: Comprehensive metabolic panel interpretation — electrolytes, kidney, and liver (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: Mild hyponatremia (130 to 134) is often dilutional in the outpatient setting, related to thiazides, SSRIs, or excess water intake; sodium below 125 with neurologic symptoms is a medical emergency requiring careful correction at no more than 8 to 10 mEq/L per 24 hours to avoid osmotic demyelination. Hypernatremia (sodium above 145) most commonly reflects free-water deficit and is corrected with hypotonic fluid at a similarly cautious rate. Serum potassium of 5.5 mEq/L or higher is hyperkalemia and warrants action: confirm with a non-hemolyzed sample, obtain an ECG when above 6.0 or with any conduction abnormality, and review medications including ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.
chunks.title: Comprehensive metabolic panel interpretation — electrolytes, kidney, and liver (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: Hypernatremia (sodium above 145) most commonly reflects free-water deficit and is corrected with hypotonic fluid at a similarly cautious rate. Serum potassium of 5.5 mEq/L or higher is hyperkalemia and warrants action: confirm with a non-hemolyzed sample, obtain an ECG when above 6.0 or with any conduction abnormality, and review medications including ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics, and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Persistent potassium above 6.0 mEq/L or any value with ECG changes is treated emergently with calcium, intracellular shift agents (insulin with glucose, beta-agonists), and removal therapy.

Hypokalemia below 3.5 mEq/L is often diuretic- or vomiting-related; replace and assess for ongoing losses, particularly in patients on digoxin or with cardiac arrhythmia risk.

Estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) below 60 mL/min/1.73 m² persisting for at least three months establishes chronic kidney disease; an isolated low eGFR may reflect acute illness, dehydration, recent contrast, or laboratory variability and should be confirmed before staging. A BUN-to-creatinine ratio above about 20 favors a pre-renal pattern (volume depletion, GI bleed, high-protein intake) over intrinsic renal disease. AST or ALT elevations are interpreted relative to upper limit of normal: less than 5 times upper limit is mild, 5 to 10 times is moderate, and greater than 10 times is severe.

chunks.text: A BUN-to-creatinine ratio above about 20 favors a pre-renal pattern (volume depletion, GI bleed, high-protein intake) over intrinsic renal disease. AST or ALT elevations are interpreted relative to upper limit of normal: less than 5 times upper limit is mild, 5 to 10 times is moderate, and greater than 10 times is severe. ALT above AST favors non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and chronic hepatitis; AST above ALT in a 2-to-1 or higher ratio favors alcohol-related liver disease.

chunks.text: AST or ALT elevations are interpreted relative to upper limit of normal: less than 5 times upper limit is mild, 5 to 10 times is moderate, and greater than 10 times is severe. ALT above AST favors non-alcoholic fatty liver disease and chronic hepatitis; AST above ALT in a 2-to-1 or higher ratio favors alcohol-related liver disease. Alkaline phosphatase rises with cholestatic processes and bone turnover; pair with gamma-glutamyl transferase to localize to the liver.

Alkaline phosphatase rises with cholestatic processes and bone turnover; pair with gamma-glutamyl transferase to localize to the liver. Glucose interpretation depends on fasting status: a fasting glucose of 126 mg/dL or higher on two occasions, or a random glucose of 200 mg/dL with classic symptoms, indicates diabetes; fasting glucose of 100 to 125 indicates impaired fasting glucose.

chunks.text: For most non-pregnant adults with type 2 diabetes, a hemoglobin A1c target of less than 7 percent is reasonable, with individualization toward 7.5 to 8 percent in older adults, those with limited life expectancy, advanced complications, or a history of severe hypoglycemia. A1c should be checked at least twice yearly when on stable therapy at goal, and quarterly when therapy has changed or glycemic targets are not being met. Metformin remains first-line oral pharmacotherapy in patients without contraindications.

chunks.text: Hashimoto's disease is a chronic autoimmune lymphocytic thyroiditis and is the most common cause of primary hypothyroidism in iodine- sufficient regions. Antibodies against thyroid peroxidase (TPO) are positive in roughly 90 percent of patients with Hashimoto's hypothyroidism, and antibodies against thyroglobulin are positive in a substantial minority. Either antibody can also be present in euthyroid relatives without active disease.
chunks.title: Hashimoto's disease — autoimmune thyroiditis and antibody testing (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: Antibodies against thyroid peroxidase (TPO) are positive in roughly 90 percent of patients with Hashimoto's hypothyroidism, and antibodies against thyroglobulin are positive in a substantial minority. Either antibody can also be present in euthyroid relatives without active disease. A single TPO antibody titer is sufficient to support an autoimmune etiology when an elevated TSH and a low or low-normal free T4 are already documented.

chunks.text: Either antibody can also be present in euthyroid relatives without active disease. A single TPO antibody titer is sufficient to support an autoimmune etiology when an elevated TSH and a low or low-normal free T4 are already documented. Repeat antibody testing has limited additional clinical value once the autoimmune diagnosis is established; titers do not reliably track disease activity or response to therapy and are not used to titrate levothyroxine.

Repeat antibody testing has limited additional clinical value once the autoimmune diagnosis is established; titers do not reliably track disease activity or response to therapy and are not used to titrate levothyroxine. Patients with Hashimoto's disease are at increased risk for other autoimmune conditions, including type 1 diabetes, Addison's disease, celiac disease, vitiligo, and pernicious anemia, so a careful review of systems and a low threshold for evaluating new symptoms are appropriate.

chunks.text: Primary hypothyroidism is diagnosed biochemically when serum thyroid-stimulating hormone (TSH) is elevated and free thyroxine (free T4) is low. Subclinical hypothyroidism describes an elevated TSH with a normal free T4; the typical reflex is to confirm the finding with a repeat TSH at 6 to 8 weeks before initiating long-term therapy. Common causes include autoimmune (Hashimoto's) thyroiditis, prior radioiodine or thyroid surgery, and certain medications.
chunks.title: Hypothyroidism — diagnosis, levothyroxine therapy, and follow-up monitoring (NIH/NIDDK)

chunks.text: Subclinical hypothyroidism describes an elevated TSH with a normal free T4; the typical reflex is to confirm the finding with a repeat TSH at 6 to 8 weeks before initiating long-term therapy. Common causes include autoimmune (Hashimoto's) thyroiditis, prior radioiodine or thyroid surgery, and certain medications. Symptoms can include fatigue, cold intolerance, dry skin, constipation, weight change, menstrual irregularity, and depressed mood; symptom severity correlates loosely with biochemical severity.

chunks.text: Insulin therapy is added in type 2 diabetes when non-insulin agents fail to achieve the individualized A1c target despite adequate adherence and dose escalation. Common triggers for adding basal insulin are an A1c that remains above 9 percent on combination oral therapy, a fasting plasma glucose persistently above 180 mg/dL, the presence of catabolic features (weight loss, ketosis, classic symptoms), or hyperglycemic emergencies. A GLP-1 receptor agonist (semaglutide, dulaglutide, liraglutide) is generally preferred over basal insulin as the first injectable in patients without symptomatic hyperglycemia or acute illness because it preserves weight benefit and is comparably effective at A1c reduction up to about 1.5 to 2 points.

When the A1c remains above target on combination metformin plus a GLP-1 receptor agonist, basal insulin is the next step.

When the A1c remains above target on combination metformin plus a GLP-1 receptor agonist, basal insulin is the next step. chunks.text: Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening for colorectal cancer in all adults aged 45 to 75 years. This is a Grade B recommendation. Adults aged 76 to 85 years should be screened selectively based on overall health, prior screening history, and patient preference (Grade C).
chunks.title: Colorectal cancer screening (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This is a Grade B recommendation. Adults aged 76 to 85 years should be screened selectively based on overall health, prior screening history, and patient preference (Grade C). Acceptable screening strategies include high-sensitivity guaiac fecal occult blood test (gFOBT) or fecal immunochemical test (FIT) annually, multitarget stool DNA every 1 to 3 years, CT colonography every 5 years, flexible sigmoidoscopy every 5 years (or every 10 years with annual FIT), and colonoscopy every 10 years.
chunks.title: Colorectal cancer screening (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Adults aged 76 to 85 years should be screened selectively based on overall health, prior screening history, and patient preference (Grade C). Acceptable screening strategies include high-sensitivity guaiac fecal occult blood test (gFOBT) or fecal immunochemical test (FIT) annually, multitarget stool DNA every 1 to 3 years, CT colonography every 5 years, flexible sigmoidoscopy every 5 years (or every 10 years with annual FIT), and colonoscopy every 10 years. Screening reduces colorectal cancer incidence and mortality.
chunks.title: Colorectal cancer screening (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Acceptable screening strategies include high-sensitivity guaiac fecal occult blood test (gFOBT) or fecal immunochemical test (FIT) annually, multitarget stool DNA every 1 to 3 years, CT colonography every 5 years, flexible sigmoidoscopy every 5 years (or every 10 years with annual FIT), and colonoscopy every 10 years. Screening reduces colorectal cancer incidence and mortality. The net benefit is substantial for adults aged 50 to 75, moderate for adults aged 45 to 49, and small for adults aged 76 to 85.

chunks.text: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening for hypertension in adults 18 years or older with office blood pressure measurement. This is a Grade A recommendation.
chunks.title: Screening for hypertension in adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening for hypertension in adults 18 years or older with office blood pressure measurement. This is a Grade A recommendation. The recommendation also calls for confirmation of high blood pressure outside the clinical setting before starting treatment.
chunks.title: Screening for hypertension in adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This is a Grade A recommendation. The recommendation also calls for confirmation of high blood pressure outside the clinical setting before starting treatment. Acceptable confirmation methods include 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (the reference standard) or home blood pressure monitoring.
chunks.title: Screening for hypertension in adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: The recommendation also calls for confirmation of high blood pressure outside the clinical setting before starting treatment. Acceptable confirmation methods include 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (the reference standard) or home blood pressure monitoring. Out-of-office measurement reduces misclassification caused by white-coat hypertension and improves cardiovascular outcomes when used to guide therapy.
chunks.title: Screening for hypertension in adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Acceptable confirmation methods include 24-hour ambulatory blood pressure monitoring (the reference standard) or home blood pressure monitoring. Out-of-office measurement reduces misclassification caused by white-coat hypertension and improves cardiovascular outcomes when used to guide therapy. Adults aged 18 to 39 years at average risk should be rescreened every 3 to 5 years; adults 40 years or older or with risk factors should be rescreened annually.
chunks.title: Screening for hypertension in adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Out-of-office measurement reduces misclassification caused by white-coat hypertension and improves cardiovascular outcomes when used to guide therapy. Adults aged 18 to 39 years at average risk should be rescreened every 3 to 5 years; adults 40 years or older or with risk factors should be rescreened annually. Risk factors include overweight or obesity, Black race, and family history of hypertension.
chunks.title: Screening for hypertension in adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends annual screening for lung cancer with low-dose computed tomography in adults aged 50 to 80 years who have a 20 pack-year smoking history and currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years. This is a Grade B recommendation.
chunks.title: Lung cancer screening (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Preventive Services Task Force recommends annual screening for lung cancer with low-dose computed tomography in adults aged 50 to 80 years who have a 20 pack-year smoking history and currently smoke or have quit within the past 15 years. This is a Grade B recommendation. Screening should be discontinued once a person has not smoked for 15 years or develops a health problem that substantially limits life expectancy or the ability or willingness to have curative lung surgery.
chunks.title: Lung cancer screening (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This is a Grade B recommendation. Screening should be discontinued once a person has not smoked for 15 years or develops a health problem that substantially limits life expectancy or the ability or willingness to have curative lung surgery. Compared to the prior 2013 recommendation, the 2021 update lowered the starting age from 55 to 50 and the pack-year threshold from 30 to 20.
chunks.title: Lung cancer screening (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Screening should be discontinued once a person has not smoked for 15 years or develops a health problem that substantially limits life expectancy or the ability or willingness to have curative lung surgery. Compared to the prior 2013 recommendation, the 2021 update lowered the starting age from 55 to 50 and the pack-year threshold from 30 to 20. The change was driven by evidence that screening reduces lung cancer mortality at lower exposure thresholds, particularly among women and Black adults who tend to develop lung cancer at lower cumulative tobacco exposure.
chunks.title: Lung cancer screening (USPSTF)

chunks.text: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes in adults aged 35 to 70 years who have overweight or obesity. This is a Grade B recommendation.
chunks.title: Screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Preventive Services Task Force recommends screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes in adults aged 35 to 70 years who have overweight or obesity. This is a Grade B recommendation. Clinicians should offer or refer patients with prediabetes to effective preventive interventions.
chunks.title: Screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This is a Grade B recommendation. Clinicians should offer or refer patients with prediabetes to effective preventive interventions. Acceptable screening tests include fasting plasma glucose, hemoglobin A1c, or oral glucose tolerance testing.
chunks.title: Screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Clinicians should offer or refer patients with prediabetes to effective preventive interventions. Acceptable screening tests include fasting plasma glucose, hemoglobin A1c, or oral glucose tolerance testing. A fasting plasma glucose of 100 to 125 mg/dL or an A1c of 5.7 to 6.4 percent indicates prediabetes; a fasting plasma glucose of 126 mg/dL or higher on two occasions, or an A1c of 6.5 percent or higher, indicates diabetes.
chunks.title: Screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Acceptable screening tests include fasting plasma glucose, hemoglobin A1c, or oral glucose tolerance testing. A fasting plasma glucose of 100 to 125 mg/dL or an A1c of 5.7 to 6.4 percent indicates prediabetes; a fasting plasma glucose of 126 mg/dL or higher on two occasions, or an A1c of 6.5 percent or higher, indicates diabetes. A single elevated random plasma glucose value is not sufficient for diagnosis without confirmatory testing.
chunks.title: Screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (USPSTF)

chunks.text: A fasting plasma glucose of 100 to 125 mg/dL or an A1c of 5.7 to 6.4 percent indicates prediabetes; a fasting plasma glucose of 126 mg/dL or higher on two occasions, or an A1c of 6.5 percent or higher, indicates diabetes. A single elevated random plasma glucose value is not sufficient for diagnosis without confirmatory testing. Patients with prediabetes benefit from intensive behavioral counseling on diet and physical activity, and selected patients may benefit from metformin.
chunks.title: Screening for prediabetes and type 2 diabetes (USPSTF)

chunks.text: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force recommends that adults aged 40 to 75 years who have one or more cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk factors and an estimated 10-year CVD risk of 10% or greater initiate a statin for the primary prevention of CVD. This is a Grade B recommendation.
chunks.title: Statin use for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Preventive Services Task Force recommends that adults aged 40 to 75 years who have one or more cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk factors and an estimated 10-year CVD risk of 10% or greater initiate a statin for the primary prevention of CVD. This is a Grade B recommendation. Adults aged 40 to 75 years with one or more CVD risk factors and a 10-year CVD risk of 7.5% to less than 10% may selectively be offered a statin.
chunks.title: Statin use for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This is a Grade B recommendation. Adults aged 40 to 75 years with one or more CVD risk factors and a 10-year CVD risk of 7.5% to less than 10% may selectively be offered a statin. This is a Grade C recommendation; the net benefit is small.
chunks.title: Statin use for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Adults aged 40 to 75 years with one or more CVD risk factors and a 10-year CVD risk of 7.5% to less than 10% may selectively be offered a statin. This is a Grade C recommendation; the net benefit is small. CVD risk factors in the underlying evidence base include dyslipidemia (LDL-C greater than 130 mg/dL or HDL-C less than 40 mg/dL), diabetes, hypertension, and smoking.
chunks.title: Statin use for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This is a Grade C recommendation; the net benefit is small. CVD risk factors in the underlying evidence base include dyslipidemia (LDL-C greater than 130 mg/dL or HDL-C less than 40 mg/dL), diabetes, hypertension, and smoking. The 10-year CVD risk estimate is calculated using the AHA/ACC Pooled Cohort Equations.
chunks.title: Statin use for the primary prevention of cardiovascular disease (USPSTF)

chunks.text: CVD risk factors in the underlying evidence base include dyslipidemia (LDL-C greater than 130 mg/dL or HDL-C less than 40 mg/dL), diabetes, hypertension, and smoking. The 10-year CVD risk estimate is calculated using the AHA/ACC Pooled Cohort Equations. For adults 76 years or older, the current evidence is insufficient (Grade I) to assess the balance of benefits and harms of initiating a statin for primary prevention.

chunks.text: The U.S. Preventive Services Task Force concludes that the current evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults. This is a Grade I statement.
chunks.title: Screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: Preventive Services Task Force concludes that the current evidence is insufficient to assess the balance of benefits and harms of screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults. This is a Grade I statement. This Grade I statement applies only to screening of asymptomatic adults who do not have clinical signs or symptoms of thyroid dysfunction and are not pregnant.
chunks.title: Screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This is a Grade I statement. This Grade I statement applies only to screening of asymptomatic adults who do not have clinical signs or symptoms of thyroid dysfunction and are not pregnant. It does not apply to follow-up of patients with prior thyroid disease, abnormal prior thyroid testing, or symptoms suggestive of thyroid dysfunction (fatigue, unintentional weight change, cold intolerance, abnormal menses, goiter), in whom evaluation with TSH is clinically indicated.
chunks.title: Screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: This Grade I statement applies only to screening of asymptomatic adults who do not have clinical signs or symptoms of thyroid dysfunction and are not pregnant. It does not apply to follow-up of patients with prior thyroid disease, abnormal prior thyroid testing, or symptoms suggestive of thyroid dysfunction (fatigue, unintentional weight change, cold intolerance, abnormal menses, goiter), in whom evaluation with TSH is clinically indicated. When TSH testing is performed, an elevated serum thyroid-stimulating hormone with a low or low-normal free thyroxine (free T4) is the biochemical pattern of overt primary hypothyroidism.
chunks.title: Screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: It does not apply to follow-up of patients with prior thyroid disease, abnormal prior thyroid testing, or symptoms suggestive of thyroid dysfunction (fatigue, unintentional weight change, cold intolerance, abnormal menses, goiter), in whom evaluation with TSH is clinically indicated. When TSH testing is performed, an elevated serum thyroid-stimulating hormone with a low or low-normal free thyroxine (free T4) is the biochemical pattern of overt primary hypothyroidism. An isolated elevated TSH with a normal free T4 is the pattern of subclinical hypothyroidism.
chunks.title: Screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults (USPSTF)

chunks.text: When TSH testing is performed, an elevated serum thyroid-stimulating hormone with a low or low-normal free thyroxine (free T4) is the biochemical pattern of overt primary hypothyroidism. An isolated elevated TSH with a normal free T4 is the pattern of subclinical hypothyroidism. Confirmation of an abnormal TSH with a repeat measurement, typically several weeks later, is recommended before a lifelong treatment decision is made.
chunks.title: Screening for thyroid dysfunction in nonpregnant, asymptomatic adults (USPSTF)
